The Air has more thermal  throttling, which will matter to mobile gamers and   those trying to use their phones in hot conditions  like shooting a bunch of video outside. The iPhone   17 Pro and Pro Max are going to be better choices  for those types of activities. Another difference   between them is storage. If you need the most  storage available on an iPhone, the 17 Pro Max   has a 2 TB configuration for $2,000, while the Air  and the Pro top out at 1 TB. Now, let's discuss   price. For the lightest phone, you'll also end  up paying a little bit less than the Pro iPhones.   The iPhone Air starts at $999 with 256 gigs of  storage, which is the starting storage that all of   this year's iPhones start at. And you can see the  current price on Amazon and check prices across   multiple retailers using the product links in the  description. So, which phone do I think you should   buy? Generally, I think most people should buy the  iPhone Air. It's sleeker looking, easier to carry   around, does have all day battery life, I think  for most people, and is less expensive. Generally,   I don't think most people will miss the extra  cameras, especially the ultrawide. I bet if   you scroll through your current phone's library  of photos, like 90% of the photos you've taken   are probably from either the selfie camera or the  main wide camera on the back. So, you have to ask   yourself, is having a bigger, heavier phone that's  more cumbersome to literally hold in your hand   worth it for the times that you want the telephoto  and the ultrawide camera? For most people, I think   that answer is probably going to be no. Now, do  I wish Apple could figure out a way to give us   a phone like the Air, but with the triple camera  system? Yes. That would be pretty amazing. Now,   if you do use the telephoto and ultrawide cameras  a lot, you need a phone with better thermals for   gaming and or want better baked-in battery life  that you carry around with you all of the time. A   phone with better external speakers and maybe one  with a slightly larger display as in the case with   the Pro Max. That's where I think you'd still go  with the Pro iPhones. So, those are my thoughts on   which iPhone I think you should buy, which one I  found myself wanting to pick up most of the time.   But, let me know if you've held the Air, what you  think of it, and which phone you're planning to   buy, and why in the comments. And make sure you're  subscribed to see both of my long-term reviews of   both of these phones. And to see more of my videos  on Apple products, you can do that by clicking   here. And then you can click here to see more of  my comparison videos like my upcoming comparison   between the S25 Ultra and 17 Pro Max. And to see  all of the products I recommend at any time, you   can do that by visiting 6months.net/shop. For 6  Months Later, I'm Josh Teder. Thanks for watching.

title: iPhone 17 - 1 Month Later Honest Review (Life After The Hype)
transcript: Now, have you ever thought that when a new iPhone comes out, it's going to be the same old story, same design, same feel, just a new name and a higher price tag? Yeah, that's exactly what I thought when I first unboxed the iPhone 17. But after a month of actually living with it, not testing it, not spec sheet comparing it, I realized that this thing isn't just another small upgrade. Judging from my personal experience, it's the first iPhone in almost 10 years that actually feels like it respects your money. It's reasonably priced. It cuts almost no corners and somehow still feels premium in every way that matters. And the wild part is that even without the extra camera and a slight redesign, when I'm using it dayto-day, you know, scrolling, editing, taking photos, or replying to text, I genuinely can't tell the difference between this and my iPhone 17 Pro Max. Besides the size, the smoothness, the display, the way it moves through apps is almost identical. And that's what makes the iPhone 17 feel much more different compared to the 16, the 15, and even the 14. Because for once, the quoteunquote regular iPhone isn't the one that you settle for, it's actually the one that you want. So, in this video, we're going deep. After one month of using it as my main phone, from long days of filming to random late night scroll sessions, I'm breaking down everything that Apple got right, everything that they still need to fix, and why this might secretly be the best value iPhone that Apple's made in a decade. But before we dive in though, if you love honest reviews with zero fluff, make sure you hit that like button, subscribe to the channel, and follow me on Twitter and Instagram for all of the behindthe-scenes updates and early impressions on upcoming tech. Now, look, not every device is perfect, and even though I've been impressed with the iPhone 17 overall, there are a few little things that I've noticed after a month that remind you that this is still tech and not magic. Now, about once every 2 weeks, I get this random glitch. You know, nothing crazy. Just small stuff like animations freezing for a second or the screen not responding right away. And honestly, it was so infrequent that it's not that bad, but it's still there. Then there's the connection. There were a few moments where I was on the phone with my dad and out of nowhere he couldn't hear me. At first, I thought it was a network thing, but after comparing it with my iPhone 17 Pro Max, I realized that the signal on the regular 17 can be a bit weaker in certain spots. It's nothing dealbreaking, but it's one of those things that remind you that even with Apple's reputation for polish, you're still going to run into some minor hiccups here and there. But aside from that, I've got to give Apple credit where credit is due. They made a genuinely great phone this year. One that checks almost every single box. The performance, the display, the build, it all just works. And again, the best part is is that the base iPhone isn't a phone that you settle for anymore. It's not the only one that you buy because you can't afford the Pro. It's the one that you buy because it's actually enough this time. Now, the iPhone 17 feels familiar, but in the best way possible. If I'm being honest, Apple did not reinvent the wheel here. Instead, they polished it. The moment you pick it up, you can tell that this phone was built to feel balanced. It's light enough to handle one-handed, but still has that reassuring weight that makes it feel premium. And after a month of daily use, pocketing it, tossing it on desks, and even using it caseless for a bit, it's held up really well. No major scratches on the back, no weird fading around the edges, and the matte finish does a great job at hiding fingerprints. The edges have this slightly curved satin feel that's easier to grip and it doesn't dig into your palm since it has smooth edges instead of a boxy frame. And I feel like when it comes to comfort and design with the 17, it's like they finally found that sweet spot between comfort and luxury. It's also incredibly refined when it comes to durability, I've accidentally dropped it once. It was a pretty hearttoppping moment. It was on concrete, but surprisingly it survived without a single dent or scratch. The ceramic shield front glass is still doing its job. And while it is a fingerprint magnet under harsh light, there's really nothing that a quick wipe with a microfiber cloth can fix. And another thing that I really appreciate about the 17 is how normal it feels in hand. The way it fits your grip, the way the buttons have a pretty nice click. I would say in terms of premiumness, the iPhone 17 does not feel like a cheap phone. So, if you're the kind of person who values how a phone feels as much as how it looks, this is one of Apple's most well-balanced designs in years. It's minimal, it's durable, and it manages to look premium without shouting about it. Now, moving on to battery life, the iPhone 17 has been surprisingly solid. In my day-to-day use, which is usually a mix of filming clips, scrolling through social media, answering emails, and watching a few YouTube videos, I've been averaging around 6 to 7 hours of onscreen time. And I still end most of my days with about 20 to 25% left. So, I would say that combined with downtime, it takes me usually around 13 to 14 hours to put my phone back on the charger. And another thing that I like that a lot of people look over is how much USBC is just way more convenient. I can use the same cable for my MacBook, my iPad, and my iPhone 17. And it's one of those small quality of life changes that you don't really realize how much you need it until you have it. I use a 30 watt charger to charge my 17. And I've been getting zero to 50% in about 25 minutes and a full charge in just over an hour. That's pretty good for me. But with Mag Safe, I did notice that something was pretty odd. It definitely felt slower than the last 2 years. Around the 80% mark, the phone tends to warm up and charging speeds dip a little bit. It's not a big issue, but it's definitely noticeable when you're in a rush. But overall though, I can confidently say this. The iPhone 17 nails battery life where it matters the most and consistency. You don't get that sudden drop off at the end of the day and you're not babysitting your charger every few hours. It's not the absolute best battery that Apple's ever made, but it's definitely one of the most reliable ones that I've used. Now, moving on to cameras. The iPhone 17 has really impressed me, but it's not perfect. You've got two 48 megapixel lenses this year, a main and an ultra wide, and for everyday use, this setup absolutely holds its own. Photos come out sharp, colors are rich but not oversaturated, and skin tones don't look yellow like on older iPhones. Apple's found that balance between clean and natural without making everything look overly processed. In daylight, this camera shines. The main lens captures depth and texture beautifully, while the ultra wide gives you those dramatic perspectives without the usual warping at the edges. And I've also been taking a ton of street photos, and honestly, I've stopped double-checking my shots. It's that reliable. Point shoot, and I'm pretty much good. But here's where things start to show some limits. First of all, it's only digital zoom, and it's very limited. Details soften, colors lose contrast, and it just doesn't hold that crisp clarity the Pro Max's telephoto lens can deliver. It's not bad, but it's definitely basic. And I'll be honest, besides the size, this was the only area that made me miss my iPhone 17 Pro Max. When you're used to that five times optical zoom, any flexibility that it gives you for framing or travel shots. Going back to the 17 feels very limiting. It's clearly not built for zoom enthusiasts. It's kind of built for everyday shooters who just want good results pretty fast. But besides that, moving on to the video side, Apple still holds the crown. This phone records some of the most stable, clean, and color accurate footage that you'll find on a nonpro device. Whether I'm walking down the street or recording product B-roll handheld, the stabilization feels unreal. It's smooth, cinematic, and free of that jitter that you get on most phones. The color science is consistent across lenses, too, which is something that not every brand gets right. You can switch from the main to the ultra wide, and the quality stays pretty good. And I will say overall, the iPhone 17 might be the best in terms of value, especially for the camera. So, my overall takeaway is that the iPhone 17's camera is dependable, natural, and seriously impressive for its price point. The zoom is its weakest link. It's kind of it's fine, but it's just not special. But for everything else, photos and videos come out looking professional with minimal effort. Now, moving on to performance, the iPhone 17 is a powerhouse in disguise. It's running Apple's new A19 chip, and even though it's not technically as efficient as the Pro models, the difference in realworld use is almost impossible to notice. And even heavier tasks like editing short clips or batch processing photos don't even make this phone break a sweat. And although I had been impressed with the performance, I still was a little disappointed that only the Pro models were getting vapor chamber cooling, especially when I expected the iPhone 17 to pretty much match almost everything. But in real world use, it really didn't heat up that much. The aluminum frame actually helps dissipate warmth surprisingly well. Sure, it does get a little warm when you're recording 4K video for extended periods or gaming for over an hour, but it never really crosses that ouch, my hands burning territory. There's no major thermal throttling, no performance dips, and no frame rate drops even in longer sessions of demanding games like Genchin Impact or Call of Duty Mobile. And for anyone wondering about benchmarks, yes, it's strong there, too. The A19 chip scores just shy of the A19 Pro and Geekbench, but in daily life, it feels identical. Launching apps, switching cameras, scrubbing through footage, it all happens with zero lag. So, yeah, even without vapor chamber cooling, Apple pulled off something really impressive. Now, one of the biggest upgrades this year, and honestly, one that was long overdue is that Apple finally gave the base iPhone a 120 Hz refresh rate. And yeah, just like I thought, in real life, it does make a difference. Scrolling through menus, switching between apps, and even just moving through your home screen, everything feels smoother. It's the kind of change that's hard to notice in short bursts. But after using it for a few days, going back to 60 Hz feels dated. That being said, though, this isn't some mind-blowing revelation. Apple's just catching up to what other brands have been offering for years, but it's a welcome improvement and in my personal opinion, it gives the iPhone 17 that premium fluidity that the base models have always been missing. The display itself is excellent. It's a super retina XDR OLED, same family as the Pro models, and it hits up to 30,000 nits of peak brightness outdoors. In practical terms, this means that you can actually see your screen in direct sunlight without cranking your brightness slider to the max. Colors look balanced and accurate. Apple's usual true-to-life tuning. Those sometimes can lean a bit warm compared to something like the Galaxy S25. And for watching videos or editing quick clips, the color consistency is great. It's not overly saturated, and it doesn't overdo contrast like some Android panels do. It's just clean, realistic, and pretty easy on the eyes. Now, when it comes to speakers, they're solid. Not mind-blowing, but definitely reliable. The stereo separation is noticeable and the vocals come through clean even at high volumes. In my personal opinion, it's great for calls, music, or even watching content without earbuds. And overall, I say that the display and the speakers on the iPhone 17 do exactly what they should. Nothing revolutionary, but nothing disappointing either. But next up, let's move on to software because honestly, that's where my relationship with the iPhone 17 started off a little rough. Now, when I first booted up this phone and used iOS 26, I'm not going to lie, I hated it. It felt like Apple was trying too hard to make everything look futuristic, and instead it came off cluttered. The new liquid glass interface looks strange to me at first. Too reflective, too busy, and I remember thinking, why can't Apple just keep things simple? But after a few weeks, it kind of grew on me. Once I learned how to actually customize, iOS 26 started to really look nice. The new home screen customization tools are easily the best part of this update. I can finally get my icons, widgets, and even the wallpaper tones to match perfectly. And it's even more convenient that I don't need a third party app. So whether I'm rocking a minimal black setup or something bright and colorful, iOS 26 lets it all blend together seamlessly. And for me personally, it took iOS 18 to an extra notch. Now, let's cut straight to the numbers. The iPhone 17 starts at 800 bucks for the 256 GB model. That's the same entry price that Apple charged for the iPhone 16 base last year, but this time you're getting more storage, 256 gigs compared to 128, and some features that used to be Pro only. So, value-wise, right off the bat, you're getting a lot for the money. And after a month with the iPhone 17, I can confidently say this, Apple finally gave the base iPhone its identity back. It's no longer the version that you settle for. It's the one that you actually want. And sure, it's not perfect. The zoom camera is average. There are still tiny software hiccups. And it's missing a few of the quote unquote prolevel bells and whistles. But in every way that matters, whether it be performance, battery life, display, or build, it nails all the essentials. This is a phone that doesn't try to impress you with specs on paper. It just quietly gets everything right in day-to-day use. So, if you've been holding on to an older iPhone and waiting for a model that feels premium without paying a pro price tag, this is it. The iPhone 17 is the most well-balanced, well-rounded iPhone that Apple's made in years. And honestly, that's not something that I thought I'd be saying about a base model. But anyway, that's been my full 1 month review. If you found this video helpful, drop a like and don't forget to subscribe because it really does help the channel grow. Now, I've been traveling a lot lately and honestly, my old luggage just couldn't keep up anymore. So, I finally decided that it was time for an upgrade, and that's when Level 8 sent over their luminous textured two-piece set. Now, right away, these felt completely different. They've got that clean matte finish that just looks right, and the whole build just feels premium. The wheels glide effortlessly, no wobble, no resistance, and both bags come with built-in TSA approved locks, so everything stays secure while I'm traveling. Inside, there's plenty of space. The carry-on fits my camera gear, chargers, clothes, and daily essentials perfectly. While the larger check bag has enough room for all of my clothes, shoes, and even some filming equipment when I'm traveling longer. And because both of these are made from German melon polycarbonate, they're both super durable, but still lightweight. And then there's the Level 8 Element toiletry case. It's water resistant, neatly organized, and it even has a hanging hook, so I can use it anywhere. So yeah, if your luggage has seen better days like mine did, the level eight luminous set and element toiletry case are the upgrades that you'll actually feel every single trip. So if you guys are interested in these traveling essentials, click the link in the description and comment section of today's video. [music] Every time. [music] Every time.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Zoom Test: Can it Beat the Sony RX100 VII?
transcript: Hi everyone, Chris here. In this video, I will  compare the zoom of the Sony RX100 VII with the   zoom of the new iPhone 17 Pro Max. On paper, the  zoom should be roughly comparable because both of   them now reach a 200 mm equivalent focal length at  maximum zoom range. But instead of talking about   the specs, I will be taking photos and videos at  the maximum optical zoom range and compare the   quality side-by-side. I expect that the Sony  camera still should show the better results   because of the larger lens and the bigger sensor,  but let's see. First, let's compare RAW photos.   Therefore, I configured the Sony camera to take  RAW photos, the JPEG quality to extra fine,   and the image size to maximum level. And  then I set the zoom to 200 mm. On the iPhone,   in camera settings, under formats, I set the  photo mode to 24 megapixels and the ProRAW   format to JPEG-XL Lossy. In the iPhone camera  app, I set the quality to RAW and 48 megapixels.   As focus point, I chose this little house on the  beach in 550 m distance. And then I took photos   on both devices at the same time. Here you can  see the complete photo taken at 200 mm by each   device. As you can see at the top, the Sony camera  has more pixels, but also the bigger file size.   Now, let's crop in to better compare  the quality. As you can see, by looking,   for example, at the solar panels, there's  less detail in the iPhone photo. Now,   let's compare videos and see if we get the  same results. Therefore, let's go back to the   camera settings and set Record Video to 4K  at 25 frames per second. And under formats,   let's activate Apple ProRes and set the encoding  to Log. Now, let's switch the Sony to video mode.   And then zoom in again and set the focus point  on the house. Then in the iPhone camera app,   let's switch to video and activate ProRes Log  4K 25 frames per second. Then switch to the   8x zoom. Adjust the camera and focus on the house  again. Now let's start recording on both devices. Okay, let's compare the video recordings.   Here are both videos in 4K side-by-side. Final  Cut Pro already automatically applied the Apple   Log to the iPhone footage, but this is the  ungraded version. And here you can see the   graded version to make it more comparable. And  I also applied some sharpening in Final Cut Pro. When we zoom in, we can see again the loss of  detail in the iPhone footage. Let me also show   you the unsharpened version of the same footage.  So, this is how the unsharpened version of the   iPhone footage would look like. Next, I also  wanted to check the Blackmagic camera app.   Therefore, I set the Sony camera again to 4K  and the record setting to the highest quality.   In the settings of the Blackmagic camera  app, I set the codec to Apple ProRes 422 HQ,   the resolution to 4K, and the  color space to Apple Log 2. Then I focused both of them to the house  again and then I started recording. Okay,   let's compare them again. First the  complete image at 4K, but this time   the iPhone footage is with Apple Log 2. And  now let's crop in again. As you can see,   the footage of the Sony camera has more detail.  Look, for example, at the solar panels again. Next, I also wanted to try  the zoom in motion. Therefore,   I went to a place where I could film  surfers riding the waves. Here you can   see an unccropped version of the full video  where I tried to follow the surfer in motion. And this is the same video cropped in. As you can see with my examples, the Sony still  has the better quality, but the iPhone is getting   close. So, what do you think? Which camera  would you take with you when traveling? Leave   it in the comments. And for more videos like this,  subscribe and I see you in the next one. Bye, bye.

Also the main lens had this Fusion feature, and the ultra-wide angle was simply 48 megapixels.  And now in the 17 all the lenses on the back are 48 megapixels and are described as fusion, so now they are supposed to be better. We will check soon if this is the case.  But as you can see, reading this data, you can assume that all lenses except the main one take better photos than [music] in the predecessor. Well, I'm curious. And if we click on 1x again, it displays 28 mm or 1.2x.  This means we are looking at a fragment of what the main lens sees, but we still have satisfactory resolution, so it is also described as full quality.  Clicking again in the same place, i.e. 1.2x, zooms in to 1.5x, i.e. to the 35mm equivalent .  And here we still use the same main lens.  Then we have 2x. This is still a fraction of what the main lens sees.  So here we have four lenses in one, and we only use the telephoto lens when we click on 5X in the case of the iPhone 16 Pro Max, and 4X in the case of the iPhone 17 Pro Max. title: Fix Slow Keyboard Typing after iOS 26 Update (ANY iPhone) - 17 (Pro, Max), 16, 15, 14, 13
transcript: Here's a quick fix for your iPhone's keyboard lag or slowness while typing on the iOS 26 update on any iPhone. The first solution is to reset the keyboard dictionary. The iPhone keyboard dictionary learns and saves the words you type often to improve autocorrect and suggestions while adapting to your personal typing style. Go to the settings app, scroll to general, then scroll down to the transfer or reset iPhone option. Tap on reset and then choose reset keyboard dictionary. Enter your iPhone lock screen passcode and verify it to reset the dictionary. That's it. Check your iPhone and reboot once. Hope this fixes your problem. Still not working? Then move to the next solution. Turn off the slow keys option if it's on and then force restart your iPhone. Go to the settings app, scroll to accessibility, and under keyboard and typing, tap on slow keys and turn off the toggle. That's it. Now, hard reboot to apply the change by quickly pressing and releasing the volume up button, then the volume down button, and finally, press and hold the power button until you see the Apple logo on the screen. Enter your lock screen passcode and check if the issue is fixed. Another important thing to check is to use Apple's default keyboard. Don't use a third party keyboard that's not compatible with the app you're using on your iPhone. Hold the globe icon key and switch to Apple's original keyboard or delete any extra keyboard you installed under keyboard settings. Swipe left to delete the keyboard from your iPhone. I hope you found this solution helpful. Don't forget to keep your iPhone and apps updated to the latest version to fix minor bugs and compatibility issues. Also, make sure your iPhone's updated to the latest iOS version. Don't forget to like, share, and subscribe for new free tech guide video updates. Got any questions? Drop them in the comments below.

title: Pitaka Sunset Aramid ProGuard Case for iPhone 17 Pro - Product Showcase #shortvideo
transcript: At first touch, the case feels like no other. Made from finely woven aramid fiber, the ProG Guard is silky smooth yet grippy, striking a rare balance between luxury and practicality. Aramid, the same material used in aerospace and military applications, is known for its exceptional strengthtoe ratio. Pitaka transforms it into a tactile masterpiece, making every interaction with your phone feel deliberate and refined. It's not just protection. It's a visual journey from sunset to moonrise woven into every thread. When it comes to drop resistance, the ProG Guard means business. Certified under the rigorous MISTD810H standard, proving its readiness for realworld impacts. Whether it's a casual slip from your hand or a fall from a desk, the Pro Guard's reinforced structure ensures your iPhone remains unscathed. Its arched corner cushioning system is a quiet engineering marvel. The four corners feature a curved hollow design that behaves like built-in air pockets. Upon impact, they flex and deform to absorb shock, dispersing energy before it reaches your phone, turning hard landings into soft landings.
